text.title: REST Best Practices and HATEOAS
text.summary: Naming conventions, filtering/sorting patterns, Location headers, response body conventions, and optional HATEOAS links.

rule.statement: Use lowercase kebab-case for multi-word resource names. Use plural nouns. Never use camelCase or PascalCase in URL paths.
rule.rationale: URLs are case-insensitive in the path by RFC, but inconsistent casing causes broken bookmarks and confuses consumers. Kebab-case is the web convention; underscores are acceptable but kebab is preferred.

rule.statement: Use query parameters for filtering (?status=active), sorting (?sort=-created_at for descending), and pagination (?page=2&limit=20 or cursor-based ?after=token). Never encode filter state in the path.
rule.rationale: Query parameters are composable and bookmarkable. Path-encoded filters create URL explosions and make caching by path impractical.

rule.statement: Return the created or updated resource in the response body for 201 and 200 responses. Include a Location header on 201 pointing to the new resource URL.
rule.rationale: Returning the full resource in the creation response allows clients to update local state without an additional GET. The Location header is required by RFC 7231 for 201.

example.code:
HTTP/1.1 201 Created Location: /users/456 Content-Type: application/json
{ "id": "456", "name": "Jane Smith", "email": "jane@example.com", "createdAt": "2026-04-26T12:00:00Z" }

rule.statement: For APIs that support client discovery, include a _links object with self, related resources, and state-transition actions. This is optional for most APIs but required for Richardson Maturity Level 3.
rule.rationale: HATEOAS enables hypermedia-driven clients that do not hard-code URLs. The value is highest in long-lived APIs with frequent state machine transitions (orders, payments).
